title: Footprint Acl Reconstruction
must_know: ACL provides anterior and lateral rotatory stability. Anatomic reconstruction restores native dimensions, collagen orientation, and insertion sites (footprints on tibia and femur).
Tibial tubercle, lateral femoral condyle, intercondylar notch
Standard via anterior medial parapatellar (catalog ID) or arthroscopic portals. Expose or work through fat pad/notch for tibial and femoral footprints. Protect patellar tendon insertion.

title: Footprint Meniscus Repair
must_know: Medial and lateral menisci: shock absorption, load distribution, stability. Zones (red-red, red-white, white-white) affect healing/repair vs meniscectomy. Root tears and ramp lesions important variants.
Joint line, tibial tubercle, femoral condyles
Primarily arthroscopic (all-inside, inside-out, outside-in). Open or mini-open via parapatellar for some posterior horn or root repairs. Protect MCL for medial, popliteal for lateral.
Primarily all-inside arthroscopic (or inside-out/outside-in). Open or mini-open via parapatellar for posterior horn/root repairs. Protect MCL (medial) and popliteal structures (lateral).

title: Footprint Rotator Cuff Repair
must_know: Rotator cuff: supraspinatus (suprascapular n.), infraspinatus/teres minor (suprascapular/axillary), subscapularis (upper/lower subscapular n.). Footprint on greater tuberosity; margin convergence reduces strain in U-shaped tears.
Suprascapular nerve
Greater tuberosity footprint, 'comma sign' for subscapularis

Mini-open: small horizontal variant of shoulder lateral (deltoid splitting) approach. Advantages: decreased deltoid avulsion risk vs open, faster rehab (no deltoid repair protection). Begin passive ROM immediately.

title: Quadriceps tendon layers (2-4), footprint, repair (transpatellar/anchors), chronic V-Y
summary: 2-4 layers (RF ant, VMO/VL mid, VI deep). Locking sutures + anchors/transpatellar; retinaculum. Chronic V-Y/graft.
must_know: Quadriceps tendon: 2-4 distinct layers (anterior rectus femoris, middle vastus medialis + vastus lateralis, deep vastus intermedius). Inserts on superior patella pole (with retinacular expansions).

Complete rupture: palpable gap, inability to actively extend knee against gravity or straight leg raise. Partial: may have intact extension but weakness/pain. MRI or ultrasound for confirmation and extent.

Repair: midline incision to knee. Freshen tendon ends. Transpatellar drill holes (3-4) or suture anchors in superior patella. Use nonabsorbable locking sutures (e.g. Krackow or Mason-Allen) in each layer/tendon. Repair medial and lateral retinaculum. Augment with cerclage wire or tape if poor tissue (avoid in young).

Chronic or retracted: V-Y lengthening (Codivilla) or quadriceps turndown, or allograft/autograft augmentation (e.g. Achilles). Postop: locked extension brace or cast 4-6 weeks, then progressive flexion; full weight bearing as tolerated with brace. Risks: re-rupture, patella fracture (transpatellar), infection, weakness, stiffness, hardware issues.
footprint: Quad tendon superior patella pole multi-layer
